hard.opp_name: Bronte Construction Opp isupp iSource P2P ANA Mar '26
hard.next_step: Next steps include reviewing 5-minute demo videos and ballpark pricing for 25–30 users, with a follow-up meeting targeted in April.
hard.ais_why:
• On the March 26 intro, Kyle Campbell engaged with the AI-driven RFI engine and autonomous negotiation pitch and asked about Procore integration for project-level spend.
• Bronte is a year-old procurement function running on Smartsheet, so the appetite is real but the org is small (25-30 users) and just starting to consolidate eight tools.
• A follow-up was placeheld for late April but the 4/23 call wasn't recorded; momentum exists but not yet at Hungry depth.

north_star_verdict.headline: Promising 26 Mar discovery with an interested procurement manager has gone cold: 74 days of buyer silence, single-threaded on Kyle with no ownership access, two failed follow-ups (23 Apr, 7 May), and Procore's all-in-one pull against a procurement-only add-on.
north_star_verdict.math: Created and qualified 26 Mar 2026; ~102 days in Qualified. Last verified buyer touch 23 Apr 2026 (74 days ago as of 6 Jul). Close date 23 Sep is 79 days out. Qualified deals for a live cycle typically reach Formal Evaluation within 30-45 days; 102 days in-stage with zero forward movement and 74-day silence is a cold stall. The Sep date is only achievable if the buyer re-engages now and commits to an evaluation timeline; on current pace it will slip multiple months.
evidence: Last buyer touchpoint 23 Apr 2026 (Clari email where Kyle asked to push the follow-up to 7 May); 74 days of silence since, no buyer re-initiation.

Kyle Campbell (Procurement Manager, sole contact) was genuinely interested on the 26 Mar 2026 discovery call ('you guys do have my interest here'; 'does seem like something that could benefit us big time; I am looking forward to it') and asked for a full requisition-to-PO workflow demo plus ballpark pricing for 25-30 users.
Two scheduled follow-ups did not materialize: the 23 Apr call was not recorded and the 7 May reschedule never happened; no Zycus re-outreach logged after that.
Single-threaded: 0 OpportunityContactRoles; ownership (the budget approver Kyle named on 26 Mar) has never been introduced or engaged.
Kyle cited fiscal year-end and inventory workload on 26 Mar as the reason for April timing; that constraint is now past yet no re-engagement has occurred.
Deal still sits in Pipeline forecast but 74-day silence with no forward step contradicts an active-pipeline placement.

metrics.narrative: Kyle Campbell articulated clear operational pain on the 26 Mar 2026 discovery call but never quantified it. Bronte's year-old procurement function runs on Smartsheet ('Excel on steroids... not actually designed for procurement, we just make it work'), which drives 'an extreme amount of administrative work' the team wants to eliminate, and the company juggles 'eight different softwares to get the job done' it is trying to consolidate to one. Construction adds union-compliance vendor screening and daily scope changes on top. No cost, FTE-hours, cycle-time, or error-rate baseline was ever stated, so the value case is qualitative only. Co-building a quantified admin-burden model with Kyle (hours/week on manual RFIs and invoice entry, spend leakage from uncontrolled buying) is a ranked move and the prerequisite for any ownership approval.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: 'extreme amount of administrative work'; 'we use 8 different softwares... trying to segregate that by going to one software'
Kyle Campbell, 26 Mar 2026: 'right now we're using Smartsheet... it's not actually designed for procurement, we just make it work'

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is unmapped and has never appeared in any conversation. Kyle Campbell (Procurement Manager) is the sole contact and is explicitly NOT the approver: on 26 Mar he said 'if it's something I think could fit into the company, I'd obviously have to present it to ownership... they'll actually wanna see it too.' 'Ownership' is the budget-holding decision authority but is unnamed in Salesforce (0 OpportunityContactRoles) and in the calls, so no name or title can be asserted. The evidence shows budget authority sits above Kyle and that no seller has reached it. Getting Kyle to name and introduce the ownership stakeholder for a short value briefing is the single highest-leverage gate to move past Qualified.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: 'I'd obviously have to present it to ownership... they'll actually wanna see it too'
OpportunityContactRole: 0 buyer-side roles on the opportunity

decision_criteria.narrative: Kyle surfaced concrete evaluation criteria on 26 Mar even though no RFP or BRD has been issued. Confirmed asks: (1) union-status filtering so eSourcing does not shortlist non-union vendors ('if the system's picking up every masonry contractor in Toronto and half of them aren't union, I'm wasting their time and mine'); (2) purchase-order-to-invoice matching ('they have to include the PO number in their invoice or we reject it'); (3) contract-spec-driven RFPs where the system reads the client spec and builds the requisition; (4) approval-workflow spend control ('we brought procurement in to maintain control of spend... I wouldn't want these guys to punch something in and money's out the door'); and (5) integration with, or coexistence alongside, Procore and Jonas. These are confirmed from the call but not yet formalized into a scored requirement set, so criteria are known but unweighted.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: union vendor filtering, PO-to-invoice matching, contract-spec RFP, approval-based spend control quotes

decision_process.narrative: The buying process is informal and owner-gated, and only partially visible. Kyle described a two-step path: he evaluates and then must 'present it to ownership,' who ultimately decides. No formal RFP/RFI round, no committee, and no dated timeline were defined; Kyle offered only a vague 'probably April' to reconvene and flagged fiscal year-end as a blocker. Only one buyer contact has ever appeared, so the approval chain, other influencers, and any procurement/legal steps are unknown. Mapping who besides ownership signs off, and pinning a concrete evaluation timeline, is required before the process can be forecast.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: 'I'd have to present it to ownership'; 'probably April would be when I'd be good to go'; fiscal year-end constraint

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have been discussed; the deal is far from paper. As a new-business full-suite deal (iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, ANA) it would eventually need an MSA plus Order Form and an SOW, but nothing about legal, procurement channel, InfoSec, or signatories has surfaced because the deal has not reached pricing or evaluation. This is expected at Qualified, not a red flag. Visibility will only come once the deal advances to a tailored demo and pricing, at which point the approval chain and Bronte's contracting standard (buyer paper vs Zycus SaaS terms) should be established.
sources: Stage = Qualified (SF); no contracting evidence in any call, task, or field

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific, operationally owned, and well evidenced. Kyle Campbell built Bronte's procurement function from scratch a year ago and owns the pain directly: manual, high-admin sourcing in Smartsheet, eight disconnected tools, and a loss of spend control ('before procurement was here it was the wild west, guys pulling the trigger on money left, right and center... that's how we bust our budgets'). He detailed the union-compliance screening burden (manually vetting which Toronto vendors are union) and manual invoice handling. The confirmed pain is manual administrative overload plus uncontrolled/maverick spend; inferred pain (from the eight-tool sprawl and construction scale) is project-level cost visibility, which Zycus does not fully address. Pain owner: Kyle Campbell, Procurement Manager.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: 'we brought procurement in to maintain control of spend... before it was the wild west'; 'extreme amount of administrative work'; union vendor screening quotes

champion.narrative: Kyle Campbell (Procurement Manager) is a developing champion who is now cooling. What has developed: genuine interest on 26 Mar ('you guys do have my interest here'; 'does seem like something that could benefit us big time'), strong domain expertise and detailed fit questions across union compliance, PO matching, contract-spec RFPs and Procore integration, and a stated intent to advocate internally ('I'd have to present it to ownership'). What is missing: any demonstrated access to or influence over ownership (he has not introduced them), any evidence he presented the collateral internally, and sustained engagement, he has gone silent for 74 days after two follow-ups failed. He is a credible operational sponsor but is single-threaded and at risk of fading; securing and re-activating him ranks before any commercial step.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: interest quotes + detailed fit questions
SF activity: last touch 23 Apr 2026 (74 days ago); 0 other OpportunityContactRoles

competition.narrative: The live competitive picture is a status-quo/consolidation contest, not a procurement bake-off. Procore is the alternative Kyle is 'looking into' because its all-in-one construction platform bundles accounting, project management and light procurement, directly serving Bronte's stated goal to collapse eight tools into one; Kyle conceded Zycus procurement is 'way more in depth' but framed adopting Zycus as 'throwing in another system,' and asked whether Procore and Zycus could integrate via plug-in. Smartsheet is the painful incumbent workflow being replaced. The strongest CURRENT threat is do-nothing inertia: after fiscal year-end passed the deal went silent for 74 days with no forcing event. We win by proving a reference-backed Procore API integration (so Zycus complements rather than competes with Procore) and quantifying the procurement-depth ROI Procore cannot match.
sources: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026: 'we're looking into Procore... it has the accounting, project manager, and a sense of procurement'; 'I'm curious if they'd be able to do almost like a plug-in'

deal_movement.summary: One substantive discovery call (26 Mar) produced real interest, then the deal stalled: buyer asked to push the 23 Apr follow-up to 7 May, which never happened, and there has been no buyer activity in 74 days. The only field moves since are internal (Division re-tagged to New Markets on 26 May, Lead Owner change 7 Apr), not buyer signals.

items.change: Type set to New Business; owner set to Bailey Erazo
Discovery call held: Kyle Campbell interested, requested full requisition-to-PO demo and ballpark pricing for 25-30 users
Kyle asked to push follow-up to 7 May (last verified buyer touch)
7 May follow-up did not materialize; buyer silent since
Internal: Division re-tagged Zycus -> New Markets

competitive_position.summary: The strongest CURRENT threat is do-nothing/status-quo inertia: after fiscal year-end passed, Bronte went silent for 74 days with no forcing event, so keeping Smartsheet and simply not deciding is the live risk (high). Procore is the strategic alternative (medium): its all-in-one construction platform serves Bronte's eight-tools-to-one goal, and although Kyle admitted Zycus procurement is 'way more in depth' (26 Mar), the convenience pull is real and he asked about a Procore-Zycus plug-in. Smartsheet is the painful incumbent workflow being displaced (low). No formal RFP or other named vendor has surfaced.

competitors.name: Do nothing / stay on Smartsheet
competitors.quote: 74-day silence since 23 Apr 2026; two scheduled follow-ups (23 Apr, 7 May) did not materialize; fiscal year-end constraint now past with no re-engagement.
competitors.how_we_win: Create a forcing function: re-engage Kyle with a quantified admin-burden proof point and a concrete workflow demo, then convert to an ownership value briefing with a decision timeline. Without urgency, inertia keeps Bronte on Smartsheet.

competitors.name: Procore
competitors.quote: We're looking into Procore... it's convenient in the sense it has the accounting, project manager, and a sense of procurement... your guys' procurement stuff might be way more in depth but it's adding another system into our network. I'm curious if they'd be able to do almost like a plug-in.
competitors.source: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026
competitors.how_we_win: Position best-of-breed, not either/or: prove a reference-backed Procore open-API integration so Zycus rides on top of Procore for procurement depth (AI vendor sourcing, autonomous RFI negotiation, OCR invoicing, contract-risk AI), and quantify the depth ROI Procore's light procurement cannot deliver.

competitors.name: Smartsheet (current state)
competitors.quote: Right now we're using Smartsheet, just Excel on steroids essentially... it's not actually designed for procurement, we just make it work. It has an extreme amount of administrative work.
competitors.source: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026
competitors.how_we_win: Use the Smartsheet admin pain as the burning platform; it is the status quo being replaced, not a defensible rival. No defense needed beyond quantifying the manual burden it imposes.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus fits Bronte's procurement-depth and spend-control needs well but is exposed on the two things that matter most to a construction buyer consolidating tools: project-level cost tracking (Jonas) and being an all-in-one platform. Union-vendor filtering and Procore integration are partial-fit and must be proven.

best_practice_check.summary: Early, single-threaded, cold deal: the win levers are re-engaging Kyle, breaking into ownership, and neutralizing the all-in-one Procore pull with proof plus quantified depth.
flags: Break the single-thread: get Kyle to name and introduce the ownership budget-holder before any pricing lands, the deal cannot pass Qualified without EB access.
Neutralize Procore's all-in-one pull by proving a reference-backed Procore open-API integration so Zycus complements rather than adds a competing system.
Convert qualitative admin-burden pain into a quantified case (hours/week on manual RFIs and invoicing, spend leakage) to justify a procurement-only tool to ownership.

items.name: Kyle Campbell
items.title: Procurement Manager
items.sentiment: Positive on 26 Mar ('you guys do have my interest here'; 'does seem like something that could benefit us big time'), but cooling after 74 days of silence and two failed follow-ups.
items.risk: Single-threaded and developing: owns the pain and evaluates, but has no demonstrated access to ownership (the budget approver) and has gone quiet. At risk of fading without re-engagement.
items.source: SF task/activity contact; Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026

champion_strength.summary: Kyle Campbell is a developing champion, currently cooling. Strong operational fit and clear interest on 26 Mar, but no demonstrated economic-buyer access, no evidence he has advocated internally, single-threaded, and silent for 74 days. Championship is at risk of fading unless re-activated.
champion_strength.source: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026; last contact 23 Apr 2026; 0 other OpportunityContactRoles

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Kyle engaged directly with the AI-driven RFI engine and the autonomous negotiation agents (0-5 autonomy) on 26 Mar and asked ML-savvy questions (learning to auto-exclude non-union vendors), so the agentic story landed with him. But it has not been carried to ownership and no ROI has been attached, so the Merlin/autonomous-negotiation value is under-positioned where the budget decision is made.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 6.0 (Moderate) matches the call: real curiosity about AI sourcing and autonomous negotiation from Kyle, but a small (25-30 user) year-old procurement function just starting to consolidate, so appetite exists without proven depth of demand.

items.detail: 74-day buyer silence since 23 Apr; deal has sat in Qualified ~102 days with no forward movement, putting the 23 Sep close at multi-month slip risk.
Economic buyer (ownership) never engaged; deal is single-threaded on Kyle who is not the approver.
items.source: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026

items.detail: Bronte wants to consolidate eight tools into one; Zycus is procurement-only, so it risks being seen as adding a system rather than reducing sprawl, exactly the objection Kyle raised.
items.source: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026

items.detail: No project-level spend tracking (Jonas gap) and unproven Procore integration precedent; both are fit gaps for a construction buyer.
items.source: Kyle Campbell + Bailey Erazo, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026

items.detail: No quantified ROI; pricing only offered as a ballpark for 25-30 users and never confirmed received or reviewed, so no value justification exists for ownership.
items.source: Kyle Campbell, Avoma discovery call 26 Mar 2026

confidence_signals.summary: Clear cooling: one substantive call on 26 Mar, then two failed follow-ups and 74 days of buyer silence with no re-outreach logged. Momentum is negative.
items: Last buyer touch 23 Apr 2026; no activity in 74 days.
Two scheduled follow-ups (23 Apr not recorded, 7 May) did not happen.

items.action: Owner re-engages Kyle directly, referencing his Procore-integration question and confirming he got the demo videos and pricing.
items.trigger: 74 days of buyer silence since Kyle asked to push the follow-up to 7 May, which never happened.
items.expected_effect: Restarts a cold thread and tests whether the deal is still live before the Sep close slips further.

items.action: Book a tailored requisition-to-PO demo showing union-vendor filtering and a Procore API plug-in.
items.trigger: Kyle asked to see the full requisition-to-PO workflow before presenting to ownership.
items.expected_effect: Delivers the buyer's core ask, proves the two fit concerns, and gives Kyle something ownership will want to see.

items.action: Ask Kyle to name and introduce the ownership budget-holder for a short value briefing.
items.trigger: Kyle stated he must present to ownership, who has never been engaged; deal is single-threaded.
items.expected_effect: Breaks the single-thread and puts the economic buyer in view, the gate to leave Qualified.

items.action: Co-build a quantified admin-burden and spend-control business case with Kyle using real Bronte project data.
items.trigger: Pain is strong but entirely qualitative; no ROI exists to justify a procurement-only tool.
items.expected_effect: Gives ownership a numeric justification and counters the do-nothing / stay-on-Smartsheet inertia.

items.action: Run a differentiation review positioning Zycus depth plus Procore plug-in against Procore-only procurement.
items.trigger: Kyle is evaluating Procore's all-in-one convenience against adding a deeper procurement system.
items.expected_effect: Neutralizes the consolidation objection and frames Zycus as complementary, not a competing system.

scope_change.detail: Full P2P suite scope unchanged since creation; no narrowing evidenced.

customer_preference.evidence: Genuine curiosity on 26 Mar but no decisive lean to Zycus; Kyle weighed Procore's all-in-one convenience ('adding another system into our network') and then went silent for 74 days, so any preference has decayed.

business_case.evidence: Strong qualitative pain (extreme manual admin, 8-tool sprawl, loss of spend control) but no cost, hours, or savings quantified.

contributions.evidence: customer requested next meeting (from call evidence).

commentary.win_position: Win 11 is carried by momentum 57 vs stage-expected 50; champion strength +0.30 (weight 15). Offsetting it: competitive strength -1.00 (weight 15).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 57 (moving forward): commercial topics entering (from call evidence). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 17 is driven by Verdict 'Off Track' — weak forward intent. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 0 is anchored on the stage win (11), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 57, and dragged by risk 17. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 2 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.title: Zycus win position
blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.
